answers.field_label: Other Goal Describe Physical Activity
answers.value:
We are working toward a building-wide goal that every student will accumulate at least 20 minutes of additional, non-PE physical activity during the regular school day, beyond recess, by the end of next school year. This will primarily be accomplished through structured classroom movement breaks and short activity-based transitions between subjects.

A second goal is to expand inclusive physical activity options for students who are not interested in competitive sports or who have physical or sensory disabilities. For example, we are piloting a lunchtime walking club that uses pedometers and a simple tracking app so students can set personal step goals and earn non-food incentives like extra library time or leadership opportunities helping set up equipment for schoolwide events.

We also want to integrate physical activity into family engagement. Our goal is to host at least two family movement nights per year (e.g., a fall "Family Fun Run/Walk" on the track and a spring "Dance Around the World" night in the gym) to model simple ways families can be active together without needing special equipment or memberships. These events will be advertised in English and Spanish, with interpreters available, so that our Spanish-speaking families feel fully welcomed and able to participate.
answers.reasoning: Pre-filled value from ground truth form

answers.field_label: Notes On Goals For Physical Activity
answers.value:
Persona/context: This form is being completed by Linda Morales, Assistant Principal and Wellness Committee Chair at Lincoln Ridge Middle School, a public 6–8 school in a mid-sized suburban district. The school serves approximately 720 students, with about 58% qualifying for free or reduced-price lunch. The school has one full-sized gym, a smaller auxiliary gym, a shared community track, and two outdoor playfields.

Current practice details:
- Recess and unstructured activity: Our 6th graders have a 15-minute outdoor recess after lunch whenever weather permits. For 7th and 8th graders, we do not have a formal recess period but encourage teachers to use the courtyard and walking paths for quick movement breaks and outdoor reading when possible. We are exploring options to build in a short structured activity break for 7th grade next year.

- Before/after-school activities: We currently run morning intramural basketball twice a week (open to all grades), an after-school running club in the fall and spring, and a general fitness club in the winter that mixes yoga, light strength training, and low-impact cardio. We also have interscholastic teams (cross country, track, soccer, volleyball, and basketball). Participation is strong among students who are already athletic, but we are trying to recruit more students who do not typically see themselves as "sports kids" by emphasizing fun, non-competitive options.

- Classroom movement breaks: Many teachers are informally using 3–5 minute brain breaks (stretching, quick aerobic activities, or short walk-and-talk pair activities) roughly once per class period, especially in 6th grade. We provided sample routines and a list of online resources at the August staff in-service. Our goal is to formalize an expectation of at least one movement break in every 55-minute class, especially for core subjects.

- Discouraging extended inactivity: Our master schedule was designed so that students do not have more than two back-to-back seated academic classes without at least a passing period, restroom break, or brief movement activity. Counselors and administrators pay special attention to students with multiple support classes (e.g., reading intervention plus math lab) to ensure they are not sitting for too many consecutive periods.

- Safety and environment: The outdoor playfields and blacktop areas are inspected monthly by the custodial staff, and any hazards (e.g., uneven surfaces, broken equipment) are reported via our district work-order system. Supervising staff receive annual training on active supervision and inclusive play, including strategies for engaging students who tend to sit alone on the sidelines.

- Walking/biking to school: We are located within walking distance for a significant portion of our students, but busy streets and limited sidewalks present challenges. To encourage safe walking and biking, we participate in the annual "Walk & Roll to School Day" and send home safety tips in English and Spanish. We also worked with the city to get an additional crosswalk painted and crossing-guard hours slightly extended. Bike racks were added last year, and we now see 15–25 bikes on a typical fall or spring day.

- Use of facilities by families: Our track and fields are open to the community until dusk when not in use for school events. Families regularly use the track in the evenings and on weekends. We posted clear signage in English and Spanish about hours, rules (e.g., no dogs on the field), and contact information. The PTA helped promote this as a free local place for families to walk and play.

Areas needing improvement:
- Although our written wellness policy clearly states that physical activity should not be withheld as punishment, some classroom teachers still occasionally take away recess or movement breaks for repeated behavior issues or incomplete work. We are addressing this through professional development, coaching, and gentle reminders. We also added this topic to our monthly staff newsletter with alternative strategies (e.g., restorative conversations, positive behavior supports) that do not involve limiting physical activity.

- We have strong partnerships with the local YMCA and Parks & Recreation Department, but we need more systematic communication with families about low-cost or scholarship-based activity programs, particularly for families who may not have transportation or who speak primarily Spanish at home. Our family liaison is working on a one-page bilingual handout listing local resources, including bus routes where relevant.

- Students with mobility limitations or chronic health conditions sometimes feel left out of informal games at recess or after school. Our PE staff is helping create a lending bin of adaptive equipment (e.g., lighter balls, scoop ball sets, soft flying discs) and teaching student leaders simple inclusive games. Over the next year, we plan to track participation more carefully to ensure students with IEPs and 504 plans are being supported in non-PE physical activity as well.

Overall, we are in a strong position with multiple opportunities for students to be active throughout the day and beyond, but we are still working on consistency across all classrooms and ensuring equity of access for less athletic students and those with disabilities.

answers.field_label: Other Goal Describe Physical Education
answers.value:
We have set a specific goal to increase the percentage of students who report feeling confident participating in physical activity outside of school from 62% (based on last year’s student wellness survey) to at least 75% within two years. To do this, we are emphasizing transferable skills—such as how to create a simple personal fitness plan, how to safely warm up and cool down, and how to modify games so that they can be played with smaller groups or limited equipment (for example, adapting volleyball skills to a game using a balloon or beach ball in a small backyard or living room).

Another goal is to deepen the integration between our PE curriculum and our health education units, especially around topics like stress management, sleep, and screen time. For example, when students learn about the impact of stress on the body in health class, PE teachers will intentionally include and label activities like yoga, breathing exercises, and light aerobic circuits as stress-management strategies, not just "exercise." We want students to leave middle school understanding that movement is a practical tool for their emotional and mental health, not just a way to perform in sports.

We are also piloting a "Student Assistant Coach" program for 8th graders who are particularly interested in leadership and physical activity but may or may not be top athletes. Selected students help with equipment setup, lead warm-ups, and support peers who are struggling with skills. They receive basic training on communication, inclusion, and safety from our PE staff. Our long-term goal is for these students to serve as positive role models, especially for younger students who might otherwise disengage from PE.
answers.reasoning: Pre-filled value from ground truth form

answers.field_label: Notes On Goals For Physical Education
answers.value:
Our school currently offers PE to all students every other day for a full 55-minute period, which means that over the course of the year, students receive a substantial amount of structured physical education aligned with state standards. The curriculum was fully revised three years ago when the state updated its physical education standards, and we participated in the district-level curriculum adoption process.

Curriculum and instruction:
- Content areas: Our PE program includes fitness education, individual and dual sports (e.g., badminton, pickleball, tennis skills), team sports (e.g., basketball, soccer, volleyball, flag football), rhythmic activities (e.g., line dance, basic hip-hop routines), and lifetime activities (e.g., walking for fitness, yoga, basic resistance training, and introductory outdoor education concepts).

- Lifelong physical activity focus: Each unit includes at least one lesson explicitly connecting the activity to lifelong health. For example, during the walking/jogging unit, students learn how to use perceived exertion scales, how to set realistic interval goals, and how to plan a 20–30 minute walking route in their own neighborhood or apartment complex. During the strength and conditioning unit, students use resistance bands (which are relatively inexpensive and easy to store at home) and learn simple bodyweight exercises that can be done in a small space without equipment.

- Assessment: We use a local standards-based assessment system that includes skill rubrics (e.g., dribbling, passing, and shooting in basketball; forehand and backhand in racket sports), fitness knowledge quizzes (e.g., understanding heart-rate zones, warm-up/cool-down principles), and self-reflection checklists. Fitness testing (e.g., PACER test, curl-ups, push-ups, sit-and-reach) is used primarily for personal goal-setting rather than grading. Students compare their own results over time rather than being compared against peers.

Inclusion and accommodations:
- Students with documented medical conditions or disabilities have individualized accommodations. For example, one student with asthma is allowed additional breaks and can walk instead of run during cardiovascular activities, while another student with a mobility impairment participates in modified games using a lighter ball and a smaller playing area. PE teachers collaborate with special education staff and the school nurse to ensure modifications are appropriate and clearly documented.

- Our teachers are intentional about offering non-competitive roles and activities. During team sports units, students may rotate through roles such as scorekeeper, equipment manager, or assistant coach so that students who experience anxiety about performance can still be engaged and successful.

Staffing and professional development:
- All PE classes are taught by two certified Health and PE teachers: Mr. Jason Kim (10 years of experience) and Ms. Rachel O’Donnell (7 years of experience). Both hold current state teaching licenses with endorsements in Health and Physical Education.

- The PE department participates in at least two district-provided professional development sessions per year focused on PE-specific topics (e.g., integrating heart-rate monitors, trauma-informed practices in PE, and inclusive strategies for students with autism). Additionally, they attend at least one external workshop or conference every other year. This year, they plan to attend a state association workshop on culturally responsive teaching in physical education.

Facilities and equipment:
- Our main gym is full-sized and can be divided into two teaching stations when needed. The auxiliary gym is smaller but adequate for small-group activities and fitness circuits. We also have access to an outdoor track and two grass fields, which are used heavily in fall and spring.

- Equipment has improved significantly in the past three years thanks to a small grant and a PTA fundraiser. We now have enough basketballs, volleyballs, and soccer balls for students to work in pairs or small groups, plus a class set of jump ropes, resistance bands, and lightweight training bars. We also purchased a sound system so that rhythmic and dance units can be taught more effectively.

Class size and safety:
- PE class sizes are comparable to our larger academic classes, generally between 26 and 32 students per section. This is manageable given our facilities, and teachers use station-based instruction to keep students active and supervised. Emergency procedures (e.g., for injuries, severe weather, or lockdowns) are practiced and posted in both gyms.

Discipline and punishment practices:
- Our written wellness policy and departmental norms clearly state that physical activity is not to be used as a punishment (e.g., extra laps for misbehavior) and is not to be withheld as a punishment (e.g., benching a student for the entire class unless there is a safety concern). Instead, behavior issues are addressed through restorative conversations, brief time-outs for reflection (while still allowing participation once the student is ready), and, if needed, referrals to administration. New staff are specifically trained on this policy, and our current PE teachers model it consistently.

Areas for growth:
- We would like to incorporate more student voice into unit planning, especially in 8th grade. This year, we piloted a unit where students choose between three "pathways" (e.g., fitness, team games, or dance) for a three-week period. Early feedback is positive, and we plan to refine this approach to allow for more personalization while still ensuring all standards are met.

- We also want to improve communication with families about what is happening in PE. Many parents primarily hear about PE when there is a uniform issue or a behavior concern. Our goal is to send a brief quarterly PE newsletter (in English and Spanish) with unit highlights, at-home activity ideas, and reminders about the connection between physical activity, sleep, and academic performance.

Overall, our PE program is strong and aligned with state expectations, with a clear emphasis on lifelong health, inclusion, and student engagement. We are continuing to refine our assessment practices and to build stronger connections between what students learn in PE and the choices they make about physical activity outside of school.
